content.text: RLHF: The SFT → RM → RL Pipeline

Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is a three-stage training recipe that turns a pre-trained language model into a model aligned with human preferences. The three stages are Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Reward Model (RM) training, and Reinforcement Learning (RL) policy optimization. Each stage has its own data, loss function, and output artifact — and each must succeed independently before the next can build on it.

InstructGPT (OpenAI, 2022) formalized this pipeline and showed that a 1.3B parameter RLHF model was preferred by human evaluators over a 175B baseline GPT-3 model 71% of the time — demonstrating that alignment quality can outweigh raw scale.

attrs.alt: Three-stage RLHF pipeline: SFT on demonstration data → RM training on pairwise preferences → PPO policy optimization. Each stage produces a checkpoint validated before proceeding.

content.text: SFT teaches the pre-trained model to follow instructions. Without SFT, a base LM (trained only to predict the next token) generates plausible continuations of prompts rather than helpful responses — it completes "Write a poem about trees" by generating more instructions rather than the poem.

The SFT dataset consists of (prompt, ideal response) pairs authored or curated by humans. Quality matters more than quantity: 10,000 high-quality demonstrations typically outperform 100,000 mediocre ones.
cover all task types the model will encounter — QA, summarization, code, creative writing, multi-turn conversation, math.
responses should follow consistent quality standards so the model doesn't learn contradictory formats.
responses should be the length a good human expert would write, not padded or truncated.

from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, TrainingArguments
from trl import SFTTrainer, SFTConfig

model_id = "meta-llama/Llama-3.2-3B"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
model     = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_id, torch_dtype="bfloat16")
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

# SFT dataset — must have a 'text' column or a formatting function
sft_ds = load_dataset("HuggingFaceH4/ultrachat_200k", split="train_sft[:50000]")

def format_chat(example):
    return {
        "text": tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
            example["messages"], tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=False
        )
    }

sft_ds = sft_ds.map(format_chat)

sft_config = SFTConfig(
    output_dir="./sft-output",
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,   # effective 32
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    num_train_epochs=3,
    max_seq_length=2048,
    bf16=True,
    logging_steps=50,
    save_strategy="epoch",
)

trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=sft_config,
    train_dataset=sft_ds,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./sft-final")
SFT loss is standard cross-entropy over response tokens only — the prompt tokens are masked. This ensures the model learns to generate responses, not to predict the prompt.
Before proceeding to RM training, verify the SFT model on:
two-round conversational quality (GPT-4 judge); target 5.0+ for a 3B model.
win rate against text-davinci-003; reasonable baseline is 30–60%.
MMLU (knowledge), HumanEval (code), GSM8K (math) — SFT should not degrade base model accuracy on these.

The RM learns to assign higher scores to preferred responses. It is initialized from the SFT checkpoint — not the base model — because the SFT checkpoint already understands the response format, making it a better starting point for quality scoring.

from transformers import AutoModelForSequenceClassification
from trl import RewardConfig, RewardTrainer
from datasets import load_dataset

sft_ckpt = "./sft-final"   # from Stage 1
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(sft_ckpt)
# num_labels=1 → single-value regression head (the scalar reward)
rm_model  = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    sft_ckpt, num_labels=1, torch_dtype="bfloat16"
)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token
rm_model.config.pad_token_id = tokenizer.eos_token_id

# Preference dataset: needs 'chosen' and 'rejected' columns
pref_ds = load_dataset("Anthropic/hh-rlhf", split="train")

rm_config = RewardConfig(
    output_dir="./rm-output",
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    num_train_epochs=1,
    max_length=1024,
    bf16=True,
    eval_strategy="steps",
    eval_steps=500,
)

rm_trainer = RewardTrainer(
    model=rm_model,
    args=rm_config,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    train_dataset=pref_ds,
)
rm_trainer.train()
rm_trainer.save_model("./rm-final")
Measure held-out preference accuracy before starting PPO. If accuracy is below 65–70%, the RM is too noisy to drive reliable policy improvement. Common causes:
Check data format; RM may not see EOS token
Insufficient data or too many noisy labels
Clean dataset; add more high-quality pairs
Proceed; monitor KL divergence closely in PPO
Reduce epochs; add label smoothing; more data

PPO fine-tunes the SFT policy using the RM as a reward signal. Four models are loaded: the policy (being trained), a frozen copy of the SFT model as reference (for KL), the frozen RM (for rewards), and a critic value model (for advantage estimation).

from trl import PPOConfig, PPOTrainer, AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead
from datasets import load_dataset
import torch

sft_ckpt = "./sft-final"
rm_ckpt  = "./rm-final"

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(sft_ckpt)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

# Policy (trained) + value head
policy    = AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead.from_pretrained(
    sft_ckpt, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16
)
# Reference (frozen SFT copy)
ref_model = AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead.from_pretrained(
    sft_ckpt, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16
)
# Reward model (frozen)
from transformers import AutoModelForSequenceClassification
reward_model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    rm_ckpt, num_labels=1, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16
).eval()

# Prompt-only dataset for rollouts
prompt_ds = load_dataset("HuggingFaceH4/ultrachat_200k", split="train_sft[:5000]")

ppo_config = PPOConfig(
    learning_rate=1e-5,
    batch_size=64,
    mini_batch_size=16,
    ppo_epochs=4,
    kl_penalty="kl",
    init_kl_coef=0.02,
    adap_kl_ctrl=True,
    target_kl=0.01,
    cliprange=0.2,
    output_dir="./ppo-output",
)

ppo_trainer = PPOTrainer(
    config=ppo_config,
    model=policy,
    ref_model=ref_model,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
)

def score_responses(prompts, responses):
    """Score (prompt+response) pairs with the frozen reward model."""
    texts = [p + r for p, r in zip(prompts, responses)]
    enc = tokenizer(texts, return_tensors="pt", padding=True, truncation=True,
                    max_length=1024).to("cuda")
    with torch.no_grad():
        scores = reward_model(**enc).logits.squeeze(-1)
    return [s.item() for s in scores]

# Training loop (simplified)
for batch in ppo_trainer.dataloader:
    prompt_ids = batch["input_ids"]
    response_ids = ppo_trainer.generate(
        prompt_ids, max_new_tokens=256, do_sample=True, temperature=0.9
    )
    prompts  = tokenizer.batch_decode(prompt_ids,   skip_special_tokens=True)
    responses = tokenizer.batch_decode(response_ids, skip_special_tokens=True)
    rewards  = score_responses(prompts, responses)
    stats    = ppo_trainer.step(prompt_ids, response_ids, rewards)
PPO is more fragile than SFT or RM training. Watch these signals:
Plateau or decline: RM may be hacked
Rapid length growth: verbosity reward hacking
> 0.3: policy updates too large; reduce LR or increase β
Collapse to zero: deterministic outputs; raise entropy coeff
Diverging: critic instability; check value coeff

Log all metrics to Weights & Biases or TensorBoard. The most important early warning is KL divergence: if it exceeds 0.15 nats/token in the first 500 steps, reduce the learning rate or increase β immediately — at high KL the reward model scores become unreliable because the policy is out of its training distribution.
After PPO, the final aligned model should be compared to the SFT baseline on both automated and human evaluations:
use the reward model or a separate judge model to compute the fraction of prompts where the PPO policy is preferred over the SFT baseline. Target: > 60% win rate.
re-run MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K on the PPO model. PPO should not significantly degrade capability benchmarks. A drop > 5% on any benchmark suggests reward hacking.
test on known red-team prompts. The RM may not have covered all harm categories — check explicitly.
ultimately, have humans rate responses from the SFT vs PPO models side by side on a representative prompt sample.

import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, pipeline

def win_rate_eval(
    policy_model_path: str,
    sft_model_path: str,
    reward_model_path: str,
    eval_prompts: list[str],
    n_samples: int = 500,
) -> float:
    """
    Compute policy win rate over SFT baseline using reward model as judge.
    Returns fraction of eval_prompts where policy is preferred over SFT.
    """
    from transformers import AutoModelForSequenceClassification
    import random

    tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(policy_model_path)
    tok.pad_token = tok.eos_token

    policy_pipe = pipeline("text-generation", model=policy_model_path,
                           tokenizer=tok, device_map="auto")
    sft_pipe    = pipeline("text-generation", model=sft_model_path,
                           tokenizer=tok, device_map="auto")
    rm = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
        reward_model_path, num_labels=1
    ).eval()

    wins = 0
    sample = random.sample(eval_prompts, min(n_samples, len(eval_prompts)))
    for prompt in sample:
        r_policy = policy_pipe(prompt, max_new_tokens=256, do_sample=False)[0]["generated_text"]
        r_sft    = sft_pipe(prompt, max_new_tokens=256, do_sample=False)[0]["generated_text"]

        def score(text):
            enc = tok(text, return_tensors="pt", truncation=True, max_length=1024)
            with torch.no_grad():
                return rm(**enc).logits.item()

        if score(r_policy) > score(r_sft):
            wins += 1

    return wins / len(sample)

win_rate = win_rate_eval(
    "./ppo-output", "./sft-final", "./rm-final",
    eval_prompts=["Explain quantum entanglement.", "Write a Python web scraper.", ...],
)
print(f"PPO win rate: {win_rate:.1%}")
Must hold 4 models; DeepSpeed ZeRO Stage 3 recommended

PPO's four-model memory requirement makes it the most expensive stage. For a 7B model in bfloat16, each model copy is ~14 GB — four copies require 56 GB before activations, optimizer states, or gradients. DeepSpeed ZeRO Stage 3 shards all of these across GPUs. DPO eliminates the critic and reward model, dropping the requirement to two model copies (~28 GB).
When to Use DPO Instead of PPO
DPO has largely replaced PPO for many production use cases because it requires only two models, no online rollouts, and is more stable. Prefer DPO when:
You have a high-quality offline preference dataset
from the policy's own distribution (or close to it).
— DPO trains at SFT cost, not PPO cost.
The task is instruction-following, chatting, or summarization
— these are well-served by offline pairs.
The reward signal is dense and well-calibrated
— code execution, math verification, or a reliable RM.
— long-horizon reasoning, multi-step tool use where online rollouts surface diverse trajectories.
The preference dataset is old or off-policy
— online PPO generates fresh on-policy pairs automatically.

RLHF is a three-stage pipeline: SFT teaches instruction-following on demonstration data; RM training learns a scalar proxy for human preference from labeled pairs; PPO optimizes the SFT policy against the RM reward while keeping KL divergence small. Each stage has a validation gate (SFT: MT-Bench; RM: preference accuracy ≥ 65%; PPO: win rate and KL monitoring) that prevents errors from propagating forward. DPO offers a simpler two-model alternative to PPO that is now the default for instruction-tuned models in resource-constrained settings. Together these three stages define the dominant method for producing aligned, instruction-following language models.
